subject: Physics
q: The radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>nd</mi></msup></math> orbit of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> of Bohr's model is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and that of fourth orbit of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Be</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> is represented as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Now the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
question: The radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>nd</mi></msup></math> orbit of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> of Bohr's model is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and that of fourth orbit of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Be</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> is represented as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Now the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
The formula to calculate the radius r of any nth Bohr's orbit is given by

r=r0n2Z   ...1

where, r0 is Bohr's radius, n is the principal quantum number and Z is the atomic number.

Thus, for Helium ion,

r1=r0222= 2r0   ...2

And, for the Beryllium ion,

r2=r0424=4r0   ...3

Divide equation (3) by equation (2) to obtain the required ratio.

r2r1=4r02r0=2

Hence, x=2.

subject: Physics
q: For a hydrogen atom, the ratio of the largest wavelength of Lyman series to that of the Balmer series is.
question: For a hydrogen atom, the ratio of the largest wavelength of Lyman series to that of the Balmer series is.
solution:
$\mathrm {Lyman}$

$\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{4}\right]=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{4}$
$\lambda_1=\frac{4}{3 R}$ ________...(1)
and $\mathrm {Balmer}$
$\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right]=\frac{5 \mathrm{R}}{36}$
$\lambda_2=\frac{36}{5 R}$
Then, $\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{5}{27}$

subject: Physics
q: In a hydrogen like ion, the energy difference between the $2^{\text {nd }}$ excitation energy state and ground is 108.8 eV. The atomic number of the ion is
question: In a hydrogen like ion, the energy difference between the $2^{\text {nd }}$ excitation energy state and ground is 108.8 eV. The atomic number of the ion is
solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{E}=13.6 \mathrm{z}^2\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right]$
$(13.6) \mathrm{z}^2\left[\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{9}\right]=108.8$
$\frac{(13.6)(8)}{9}\left(z^2\right)=108.8$
$\mathrm{z}=3$

subject: Physics
q: The minimum energy required by a hydrogen atom in ground state to emit radiation in Balmer series is nearly :
question: The minimum energy required by a hydrogen atom in ground state to emit radiation in Balmer series is nearly :
solution:
For Balmer series, transition is from n=3,4,5,6, etc to n=2.

For transition to n=2, the minimum state to transition from is n=3. 

Therefore, energy required ΔE=E3-E1=-13.69--13.6=12.1eV

subject: Physics
q: A $12.5 \mathrm{eV}$ electron beam is used to bombard gaseous hydrogen at room temperature. It will emit:
question: A $12.5 \mathrm{eV}$ electron beam is used to bombard gaseous hydrogen at room temperature. It will emit:
options: 2 lines in the Lyman series and 1 line in the Balmar series
1 line in the Lyman series and 2 lines in the Balmar series
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} &\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda} \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{12.5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\ &=993 \mathrm{~A}^{\circ} \\ &\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right) \\ & \end{aligned} $$ (where Rydberg constant , $\mathrm{R}=1.097 \times 10^7$ ) or, $\frac{1}{993 \times 10^{-10}}=1.097 \times 10^7\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right)$ Solving we get $\mathrm{n}_2=3$ Spectral lines Total number of spectral lines $=3$ Two lines in Lyman series for $\mathrm{n}_1=1, \mathrm{n}_2=2$ and $\mathrm{n}_1=1, \mathrm{n}_2=3$ and one in Balmer series for $\mathrm{n}_1=2, \mathrm{n}_2=3$

For this atom, the radius of the $n^{\text {th }}$ orbit in terms of the Bohr radius $\left(a_0=\frac{\varepsilon_0 h^2}{m \pi e^2}\right)$ is :

q: An electron in a hydrogen atom makes a transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and emits a photon. This photon strikes a doubly ionized lithium atom which was already in an excited state and completely removes the orbiting electron. The least quantum number for the excited state of the lithium-ion for the process is
question: An electron in a hydrogen atom makes a transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and emits a photon. This photon strikes a doubly ionized lithium atom which was already in an excited state and completely removes the orbiting electron. The least quantum number for the excited state of the lithium-ion for the process is
solution:
Let us assume that the electron in the lithium-ion is knocked off from the nth energy level, then

RZH2112−122=RZLi2n2

R12×34=R321n2

34=9n2

n=12≈3.5

Hence the least quantum number must be 4.

subject: Physics
q: A light of energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> is incident on a hydrogen atom in its ground state. The atom absorbs the radiation and reaches to one of its excited states. The angular momentum of the atom in the excited state is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>π</mi></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>17</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>eVs</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______ (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>eVs</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A light of energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> is incident on a hydrogen atom in its ground state. The atom absorbs the radiation and reaches to one of its excited states. The angular momentum of the atom in the excited state is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>π</mi></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>17</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>eVs</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______ (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>eVs</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Let the electron jumped to nth excited state.

In the ground state, energy E=-13.6 eV

So, using relation 12.75=13.6112-1n2

⇒0.9375=1-1n2⇒n2=16

⇒n=16=4

Now, angular momentum, L=nh2π=4h2π=2hπ

 =2π×4.14×10-15

=828×10-17π eVs

Hence, the value of x=828.

subject: Physics
q:
Read the following statements:
(A) Volume of the nucleus is directly proportional to the mass number.
(B) Volume of the nucleus is independent of mass number.
(C) Density of the nucleus is directly proportional to the mass number.
(D) Density of the nucleus is directly proportional to the cube root of the mass number.
(E) Density of the nucleus is independent of the mass number.

Choose the correct option from the following options.
question:
Read the following statements:
(A) Volume of the nucleus is directly proportional to the mass number.
(B) Volume of the nucleus is independent of mass number.
(C) Density of the nucleus is directly proportional to the mass number.
(D) Density of the nucleus is directly proportional to the cube root of the mass number.
(E) Density of the nucleus is independent of the mass number.

Choose the correct option from the following options.
solution:
Radius of nucleus is R=R0A13, where, R0=1.2×10-15 m and A is mass number of nucleus.

Thus, R∝A13

Volume of nucleus is V=43πR3⇒V∝R3⇒V∝A.

Now, the density of nucleus is given by ρ=MV=mA43πR03A=m43πR03

Thus, density is independent of A.

Hence, only A and E are correct.

subject: Physics
q: In a Frank - Hertz experiment, an electron of energy <math><mn>5.6</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi></math> passes through mercury vapour and emerges with an energy <math><mn>0.7</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum wavelength of photons emitted by mercury atoms is close to:
question: In a Frank - Hertz experiment, an electron of energy <math><mn>5.6</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi></math> passes through mercury vapour and emerges with an energy <math><mn>0.7</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum wavelength of photons emitted by mercury atoms is close to:
solution:
When electron pass through the mercury vapor, it losses some of its energy. The loss in KE of electron =5⋅6-0.7eV =4.9 eV

∴ energy of radiation emitted = 4.9 eV

∴ wavelength of radiation, λ=1.24 × 1044.9A≈ 250 nm

subject: Physics
q: The radiation corresponding to <math> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> <mo>→</mo> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </math> transition of hydrogen atom falls on a metal surface to produce photoelectrons. These electrons are made to enter a magnetic field of <math> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>4</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mo>T</mo> </mrow> </math>. If the radius of the largest circular path followed by these electrons is 10.0 mm, the work function of the metal is close to :
question: The radiation corresponding to <math> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> <mo>→</mo> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </math> transition of hydrogen atom falls on a metal surface to produce photoelectrons. These electrons are made to enter a magnetic field of <math> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>4</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mo>T</mo> </mrow> </math>. If the radius of the largest circular path followed by these electrons is 10.0 mm, the work function of the metal is close to :
solution:
We know that

R = m υ Bq

∴    υ = Bqr m

∴    KE = 1 2 m v 2

= B 2 q 2 R 2 2m

= 0.79 eV

Now from photoelectric equation

E = ϕ + KE

∴    ϕ = E - KE

          = 1.89 - 0.79

          = 1.1 eV

subject: Physics
q: In Bohr's atomic model, the electron is assumed to revolve in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> If the speed of electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Then the current associated with the electron will be__________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi><mo>.</mo></math> [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open=""><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>
question: In Bohr's atomic model, the electron is assumed to revolve in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> If the speed of electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Then the current associated with the electron will be__________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi><mo>.</mo></math> [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open=""><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>
solution:
Time taken to revolve in circular orbit is T=2πω=2πrv=2×227×5×10-112.2×106=107×10-16 s

Now, current associated with the electron is I=eT=1.6×10-19107×10-16=1.12×10-3A
⇒I=1.12 mA
Hence, the current is 112×102 mA.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Most of the mass of the atom and all its positive charge are concentrated in a tiny nucleus and the electrons revolve around it, is Rutherford's model.
Statement II: An atom is a spherical cloud of positive charges with electrons embedded in it, is a special case of Rutherford's model.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Most of the mass of the atom and all its positive charge are concentrated in a tiny nucleus and the electrons revolve around it, is Rutherford's model.
Statement II: An atom is a spherical cloud of positive charges with electrons embedded in it, is a special case of Rutherford's model.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate from the options given below.
options: Both statement I and statement II are false
Statement I is false but statement II is true
Statement I is true but statement II is false
Both statement I and statement II are true
solution:
Rutherford proposed that most of the mass of an atom is concentrated in a small, dense nucleus at the centre, which contains positively charged protons. Electrons, carrying negative charge, revolve around the nucleus in orbits.

In the Bohr model, electrons do not move in random orbits but have specific energy levels or shells around the nucleus. The electrons are not in a "spherical cloud" but are found in discrete orbits at fixed distances from the nucleus. Rutherford's model, on the other hand, did not specify the fixed orbits of electrons and was later refined by Bohr to explain the stability of atoms.

Hence, statement I is true but statement II is false.

subject: Physics
q: Hydrogen (<sub>1</sub>H<sup>1</sup>), Deuterium (<sub>1</sub>H<sup>2</sup>), singly ionised Helium (<sub>2</sub>He<sup>4</sup>)<sup>+</sup> and doubly ionised lithium (<sub>3</sub>Li<sup>6</sup>)<sup>++</sup> all have one electron around the nucleus. Consider an electron transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. If the wave lengths of emitted radiation are <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>λ</mi> <mn>1</mn> </msub> <mtext>, </mtext> <msub> <mi>λ</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msub> <mtext>, </mtext> <msub> <mi>λ</mi> <mn>3</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>λ</mi> <mn>4</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> respectively then approximately which one of the following is correct ?
question: Hydrogen (<sub>1</sub>H<sup>1</sup>), Deuterium (<sub>1</sub>H<sup>2</sup>), singly ionised Helium (<sub>2</sub>He<sup>4</sup>)<sup>+</sup> and doubly ionised lithium (<sub>3</sub>Li<sup>6</sup>)<sup>++</sup> all have one electron around the nucleus. Consider an electron transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. If the wave lengths of emitted radiation are <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>λ</mi> <mn>1</mn> </msub> <mtext>, </mtext> <msub> <mi>λ</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msub> <mtext>, </mtext> <msub> <mi>λ</mi> <mn>3</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>λ</mi> <mn>4</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> respectively then approximately which one of the following is correct ?
solution:
For radiation, we know that

1λ=RZ21n12-1n22

∴    λ ∝ 1 Z 2

λ 1 ∝ 1 1

λ 2 ∝ 1 1

λ 3 ∝ 1 4

λ 4 ∝ 1 9

∴    λ 1 = λ 2 = 4 λ 3 = 9 λ 4

subject: Physics
q: According to Bohr's theory, the time averaged magnetic field at the centre (i.e., nucleus) of a hydrogen atom due to the motion of electrons in the <math><msup><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> orbit is proportional to: (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo></math> principal quantum number)
question: According to Bohr's theory, the time averaged magnetic field at the centre (i.e., nucleus) of a hydrogen atom due to the motion of electrons in the <math><msup><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> orbit is proportional to: (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo></math> principal quantum number)
solution:
v∝ z n

r∝ n 2 z

B= μI 2R

I= e T = e ( 2πR v ) = e 2π v R

B= μ 0 2 e 2π v R 2

B= μ 0 e 4π z n ( n 2 z ) 2

B=μ0e4πz3n5

B∝ n −5 .

q: The first three spectral lines of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>-atom in the Balmer series are given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> considering the Bohr atomic model, the wave lengths of first and third spectral lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> are related by a factor of approximately <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. question: The first three spectral lines of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>-atom in the Balmer series are given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> considering the Bohr atomic model, the wave lengths of first and third spectral lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> are related by a factor of approximately <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>, to the nearest integer, is ________.
solution:
1λ1=Rz2122-132

1λ1=Rz2536   ...(i)

For 3rd line

1λ3=Rz2122-152

1λ3=Rz221100   ...(ii)

(ii)+(i)

λ1λ3=21100×365=1.512=15.12×10-1

x≈15

q: The mass defect in a particular reaction is 0.4 g. The amount of energy liberated is n×107 kW h, where n= _____. (speed of light =3×108 m s-1)

=0.4×10-3×3×1082

subject: Physics
q:
Radius of a certain orbit of hydrogen atom is $8.48 Ã…$. If energy of electron in this orbit is $E / x$. then $x=$ _____
(Given $\mathrm{a}_0=0.529 Ã…, E=$ energy of electron in ground state).
question:
Radius of a certain orbit of hydrogen atom is $8.48 Ã…$. If energy of electron in this orbit is $E / x$. then $x=$ _____
(Given $\mathrm{a}_0=0.529 Ã…, E=$ energy of electron in ground state).
solution:
We know $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{r}=0.529 \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{\mathrm{Z}} \Rightarrow 8.48=0.529 \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{1} \\ & \mathrm{n}^2=16 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=4 \end{aligned}$
We know $\mathrm{E} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2}$ $\begin{aligned} & E_{n^{\text {th }}}=\frac{E}{16} \\ & x=16 \end{aligned}$

solution:

solution:
Energy of an electron in nth orbit of an ion with atomic number z is, E=-13.6×z2n2 eV

for 1st excited state of Li++, z=3, n=2

E=-13.6×32n2=-13.6×94 eV

     = -30.6 eV

∴  Required energy =30.6 eV

subject: Physics
q:
In the hydrogen atom, the electron makes a transition from the higher orbit ($i$) to a lower orbit ($f$). The ratio of the radius of the orbits in given by $r_i : r_f = 16 : 4$. The wavelength of photon emitted due to this transition is _____ nm.
(Given Rydberg constant $= 1.0973 \times 10^7$ /m)
question:
In the hydrogen atom, the electron makes a transition from the higher orbit ($i$) to a lower orbit ($f$). The ratio of the radius of the orbits in given by $r_i : r_f = 16 : 4$. The wavelength of photon emitted due to this transition is _____ nm.
(Given Rydberg constant $= 1.0973 \times 10^7$ /m)
solution:
The radius of the $n$-th orbit in a hydrogen atom is proportional to the square of the principal quantum number $n$, i.e., $r_n \propto n^2$.

Given the ratio of the radii of the initial and final orbits:
$\dfrac{r_i}{r_f} = \dfrac{16}{4}$

This implies:
$\dfrac{n_i^2}{n_f^2} = \dfrac{16}{4} \Rightarrow n_i = 4 \text{ and } n_f = 2$

The wavelength $\lambda$ of the emitted photon is given by the Rydberg formula:
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{n_f^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_i^2} \right)$

Substituting the values:
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.0973 \times 10^7 \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{4^2} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.0973 \times 10^7 \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{16} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.0973 \times 10^7 \times \dfrac{3}{16}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{16}{3 \times 1.0973 \times 10^7} \text{ m}$

$\lambda \approx 4.86 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 486 \text{ nm}$

Answer: $486$

mv2r=14πϵoe2r2  ...1

mvr=h2π  ...2

On squaring (2)

m2v2r2=h24π2

On dividing both sides by r3 ,

m2v2r=h24πr3 ⇒v2r=h24π2r3m2

Acceleration= h24π2r3m2.

solution:
From Rydberg’s equation,
1λ=R1n2-1n22
1660×10-9=R122-132
1λ=R122-142
λ600×10-9=59×4×16×412
λ=488.9 nm

subject: Physics
q: Using Bohr's model, calculate the ratio of the magnetic fields generated due to the motion of the electrons in the $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $4^{\text{th}}$ orbits of hydrogen atom _______.
question: Using Bohr's model, calculate the ratio of the magnetic fields generated due to the motion of the electrons in the $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $4^{\text{th}}$ orbits of hydrogen atom _______.
solution:
The magnetic field at the center of a circular orbit is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2r}$.

The equivalent current due to the revolving electron is $I = \dfrac{e}{T} = \dfrac{ev}{2\pi r}$.

Substituting $I$ into the expression for $B$, we get $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 ev}{4\pi r^2}$.

According to Bohr's model for a hydrogen atom, the velocity of the electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit is $v \propto \dfrac{1}{n}$ and the radius of the orbit is $r \propto n^2$.

Therefore, the magnetic field $B$ depends on the principal quantum number $n$ as $B \propto \dfrac{1/n}{(n^2)^2} \Rightarrow B \propto \dfrac{1}{n^5}$.

The ratio of the magnetic fields in the $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $4^{\text{th}}$ orbits is $\dfrac{B_2}{B_4} = \left(\dfrac{n_4}{n_2}\right)^5 = \left(\dfrac{4}{2}\right)^5 = 2^5 = 32$.

Answer: $32$

q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> electron beam is used to bombard gaseous hydrogen at room temperature. The number of spectral lines emitted will be:

solution:
According to Bohr's postulates, if an electron absorbs a photon with energy equal to the difference between the energies of an excited state and the ground state, it will jump to a higher energy orbital.

Considering that the energy of the colliding electron is 10.2 eV or 12.09 eV, depending on how much energy is lost during the collision. n=3 is the maximum excited state. Hence, there are spectral lines in a total of

C23=3!2!1!=3

q: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> moves in a circular orbit in a central potential field <math><mi>U</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If Bohr's quantization conditions are applied, radii of possible orbitals and energy levels vary with quantum number <math><mi>n</mi></math> as:
question: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> moves in a circular orbit in a central potential field <math><mi>U</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If Bohr's quantization conditions are applied, radii of possible orbitals and energy levels vary with quantum number <math><mi>n</mi></math> as:
solution:
Fr=-dUdr=-kr

For circular motion

|Fr|=kr=mv2r⇒kr2=mv2 ........(i)

Bohr's quantization ⇒mvr=nh2π ....(ii)

From (i) and (ii)

m2v2m=kr2

⇒1mnh2πr2=kr2⇒n2h24π2mk=r4

⇒r=h24π2mk1/4 n1/2

r∝n

From equation (i) U∝n

KE=12mv2; PE=12kr2

E=K+U=12mv2+12kr2=kr2∝n
